detailed_units.title: Meeting the In-Laws
detailed_units.theme: Introduce yourself respectfully to extended family.
detailed_units.can_do: I can respectfully introduce myself and express gladness upon meeting extended family members.

new_grammar.explain_en: In Samar, addressing older relatives or community elders involves respectful titles. 'Apo' refers to grandparents or elders. 'Mano' (for men) and 'Mana' (for women) are used before a first name for older siblings, cousins, or in-laws to show warm respect.

examples.en: How are you, Older Brother Lito?

new_grammar.point: The Linker 'Nga' with Names and Adjectives
new_grammar.explain_en: The particle 'nga' links descriptive words to nouns, and it also appears between greeting words and time modifiers (like 'maupay nga aga'). It helps sentences flow smoothly and sound natural.

new_vocab.note: Also used generally for respected family elders.
Respectful title for an older male relative/in-law.
Respectful title for an older female relative/in-law.
Often used to express being glad to meet someone.
Complements 'asawa' (spouse/wife) learned in Phase 1.
Commonly encountered in the phrase 'makakila' (to get to know/meet).

sentences.war: Maupay nga kulop, Apo.
sentences.en: Good afternoon, Grandparent.

sentences.war: Ako hi Tom, an bana ni Maria.
sentences.en: I am Tom, the husband of Maria.

sentences.war: Kumusta, Mana Elena ngan Mano Lito?
sentences.en: How are you, Older Sister Elena and Older Brother Lito?

sentences.war: Nalipay ako makakila ha iyo.
sentences.en: I am happy to meet you all.

questions.q: Who is Tom introducing himself to?

detailed_units.title: Everyday Objects in the Yard
detailed_units.theme: Ask for common household items outside or in the kitchen.
detailed_units.can_do: I can identify and ask for basic daily tools and household items found in the yard or kitchen area.

new_grammar.explain_en: To politely ask if an item belongs to the household or is shared among 'us' (inclusive), the word 'aton' (our) is used. It softens a request for an item, making it sound communal and polite rather than demanding.

examples.en: Where is our chair? (Polite way to ask where to sit)

new_grammar.explain_en: 'Kadi' means 'come here' or 'bring here'. When paired with an object name, it is a gentle, colloquial way to say 'Please pass me that' or 'Bring that item over.'

new_vocab.note: Any seat or bench, very common in outdoor yards.
Specifically the outdoor broom made of coconut midribs (walis tingting).
Loanword from Spanish, standard across Samar.
Traditional clay jar used for storing cool drinking water.
Essential kitchen tool.
Commonly paired with kutsara.
Literally means 'come here', used to request items to be brought over.

sentences.war: Hain an aton lingkuran, Maria?
sentences.en: Where is our chair, Maria?

sentences.war: Aada ha gawas, dapit ha lamesa.
sentences.en: It is there outside, near the table.

sentences.war: Tom, alayon kadi an brayong.
sentences.en: Tom, please bring the broom here.

sentences.war: Gusto ko uminom han tubig tikang ha banga.
sentences.en: I want to drink water from the clay jar.

detailed_units.title: Where are You Going? (Kain ka?)
detailed_units.theme: Answer the common neighborhood passing greeting.
detailed_units.can_do: I can understandingly reply to the common casual greeting 'Kain ka?' with everyday neighborhood destinations.

new_grammar.explain_en: While 'hain' means 'where is something located', 'kain' means 'where are you going?'. In Samar culture, asking 'Kain ka?' is a friendly substitute for 'Hello' when passing someone on the street. It is not considered nosy; a short, general answer is standard.
To express where you are going, use 'madto' (will go there/heading to) or 'pakadto' (going towards). This is followed by 'ha' and the destination name.

examples.en: I am going to the house.
I am heading towards the store.

new_vocab.note: Used exclusively for direction of movement.
Future/progressive action of going to a distant place.
Indicates current movement direction.
Usually refers to the local neighborhood sari-sari store.
Crucial location in island towns like Daram.
Refers to fields, plantations, or inland farms.

sentences.war: Maupay nga aga, Tom! Kain ka?
sentences.en: Good morning, Tom! Where are you heading?

sentences.war: Maupay nga aga! Madto ako ha tindahan.
sentences.en: Good morning! I am going to the store.

sentences.war: Ikaw, kain ka pakadto?
sentences.en: As for you, where are you heading towards?

sentences.war: Pakadto ako ha pantalan han Daram.
sentences.en: I am heading towards the Daram pier.

questions.q: What is the meaning of the greeting 'Kain ka?'

detailed_units.title: Talking About the Weather
detailed_units.theme: Comment on heat, rain, and wind.
detailed_units.can_do: I can make simple, safe small-talk comments regarding current weather conditions like heat, rain, and wind.

new_grammar.explain_en: To emphasize a weather trait, place 'masyado' (too much / very) before the descriptive word. Unlike English, Waray doesn't need a dummy pronoun like 'it' in 'it is hot'—you simply state the description directly.
To state that it is currently raining, the root word 'uran' (rain) takes an internal repetition or infix to become 'umuuran' (it is raining right now).

new_vocab.note: Used for both weather temperatures and hot food/drinks.
Refers to that sticky, close heat typical in coastal Samar.
Can act as a noun or turn into a verb.
The active progressive form for current rainfall.
Also means air.
Used for chilly breezes, cold rain, or cold drinks.
Used to amplify adjectives.

sentences.war: Masyado mapaso yana nga aga.
sentences.en: It is very hot this morning.

sentences.war: Oo, kamapas han hangin didni.
sentences.en: Yes, the air is humid right here.

sentences.war: Aadi an uran! Umuuran na.
sentences.en: Here comes the rain! It is raining now.

sentences.war: Mahagkot an tubig yana.
sentences.en: The water is cold now.

questions.q: What happens to the weather during the conversation?

detailed_units.title: Time of Day and Simple Tasks
detailed_units.theme: Coordinate basic daily times for chores or meetings.
detailed_units.can_do: I can coordinate simple daily activities using words for 'today', 'tomorrow', and basic time-frames.

new_grammar.explain_en: 'Yana' means 'now' or 'today'. The particle 'nganay' indicates doing something first before anything else, equivalent to 'for a moment' or 'first'. It is perfect for telling someone you are performing a quick task.
To set an event for tomorrow, add the word 'buwas'. When combining it with a verb phrase, it shifts the context cleanly into the future without needing heavy grammatical conjugations.

examples.en: I will go to the farm tomorrow.

new_vocab.note: Can mean 'now' or 'today' depending on context.
Crucial time marker for future planning.
Drilled here as a standalone adverbial concept for 'just a moment/first'.
Commonly used for doing tasks, work, or chores.
Spanish loanword used for general jobs or heavy labor.
Highly valued concept during hot early afternoons.

sentences.war: Mano Lito, may trabaho ba yana?
sentences.en: Older Brother Lito, is there work today?

sentences.war: Diri, pahuway nganay kita yana nga kulop.
sentences.en: No, let's rest for a moment this afternoon.

sentences.war: An brayong dadi, magbuhat ako buwas.
sentences.en: The broom is here, I will do tasks tomorrow.

sentences.war: Maupay hira, mapahuway gihapon ako.
sentences.en: Good for them, I will rest as well.

questions.q: When will the tasks be done?

detailed_units.title: Phase 2 Review & Celebration
detailed_units.can_do: I can seamlessly synthesize identity, household requests, direction responses, weather notes, and time-frames in a natural neighborhood conversation.

new_grammar.explain_en: This lesson synthesizes all structures learned: introducing family status, using markers to point to yard items, explaining where you are heading when greeted casually, and matching it to time or weather constraints.

examples.war: Nalipay ako, pakadto ako ha tindahan yana kay mapaso.
examples.en: I am glad, I am heading to the store now because it's hot.

lessons.title: Polite Interactions Mix
lessons.grammar_focus: Family Titles and Yard Object Requests

sentences.war: Maupay nga aga, Mana Elena! Kain ka pakadto?
sentences.en: Good morning, Older Sister Elena! Where are you heading towards?

sentences.war: Madto ako ha pantalan, Tom. Masyado mapaso yana.
sentences.en: I am going to the pier, Tom. It is very hot today.

sentences.war: Oo, kadi nganay ha aton lingkuran han balay.
sentences.en: Yes, come here for a moment to our house chair.

sentences.war: Alayon, uminom kita hin tubig tikang ha banga.
sentences.en: Please, let's drink water from the clay jar.

sentences.war: Nalipay gihapon hi Apo didto.
sentences.en: Grandparent was also glad over there.

questions.q: What refreshing invitation does Tom offer Elena?
choices: To buy something at the store
To sit and drink water from the clay jar
